reasoning: This is a prebuilt binary package that fetches architecture-specific release tarballs from the official upstream GitHub repository (SaladDay/cc-switch-cli) with pinned SHA256 checksums. The package() function only installs the binary to /usr/bin with standard permissions. There is no pkgver() function, no .install hooks, no prepare/build/check functions, and no dynamic code execution, network calls, or obfuscation. Both models independently reached the same verdict with consistent and accurate reasoning. The only attack surface is the binary itself, which is opaque and cannot be statically reviewed in an AUR audit, but it is integrity-pinned to an official source.
coverage_issues: Both models reviewed only the PKGBUILD (1 file). This is appropriate for a binary package since there are no scripts or install hooks in the repo, but neither explicitly confirmed the absence of a .install file or other auxiliary files in the AUR repo tree.
false_negatives: Neither model addressed that the actual binary contents are unauditable — the package installs a prebuilt executable from GitHub releases that could itself contain malicious code. This is an inherent and accepted limitation for binary AUR packages, but it should be acknowledged rather than implied to be fully safe.
learnings: For binary packages (prebuilt -bin style), a single-file PKGBUILD review is sufficient and the 'shallow' trigger is a false alarm — the tool could detect that no other reviewable files exist in the repo and suppress the shallow warning.
Models should explicitly enumerate the AUR repo tree (e.g. confirm no .install hook, no patch files) to distinguish 'reviewed all available files' from 'reviewed only one of several files'.

Models should note the inherent limitation that prebuilt binaries cannot be statically audited, so the 'safe' verdict reflects supply-chain integrity (pinned checksums + official source) rather than binary content safety.
Both models gave well-reasoned, accurate assessments correctly distinguishing runtime AI-service communication (upstream app behavior) from supply-chain risk — this is a good pattern to reinforce.
